Code Production guidelines
Restrict all code to simple, linear control flow. The following are not permitted: Deeply nested comprehensions or generator expressions. Nesting beyond two levels — measured as the depth of nested ListComp, SetComp, DictComp, or GeneratorExp nodes in the AST; multiple for clauses within a single comprehension do not count as nesting — is not permitted. Generators and coroutines outside designated contexts. yield and async/await may only appear in functions explicitly designated as generators or coroutines in both their name and their docstring. All suspension points must be enumerated in a comment block at the function header. Direct or indirect recursion.
All loops must have a fixed upper-bound. For while loops, an explicit upper-bound constant must be present as a loop guard. It must be possible for a checking tool or reviewer to verify by inspection that the preset upper-bound on the number of iterations of a loop cannot be exceeded. If the loop-bound cannot be verified by inspection, the rule is considered violated.
Any mutable collection — list, dict, set, or other mutable sequence — that receives growth operations (append, insert, add, update, or item assignment to a key not already present) inside a loop must have a named upper-bound constant declared at the same scope as the collection. Each growth operation inside the loop must be immediately preceded by a guard check against that constant, with any violation treated as a guard-clause failure per Rule 5. The named constant must be declared at the smallest applicable scope per Rule 6.
No function should be longer than what can be printed on a single sheet of paper in a standard reference format with one line per statement and one line per declaration. Typically, this means no more than about 60 lines of code per function.
The assertion density of the code must average a minimum of two assertions per function. Assertions check for anomalous conditions that should never occur in normal execution. Since Python's assert keyword is silently disabled at runtime under -O, each assertion must take the form of an explicit guard clause: a side-effect-free Boolean test followed by an immediate recovery action. Any assertion that a static checker can prove can never fail or never hold violates this rule. A try/except block is only permitted when the error condition cannot be reliably checked in advance without duplicating the operation itself (e.g., a file-not-found during an atomic open, a network timeout); when used, the except block must follow the module's declared recovery convention and then exit. Recovery action convention. When a guard clause fails, recovery must follow one of the two conventions below. A module must declare a primary convention in a module-level comment. All internal functions must follow the primary convention. Functions that form an explicit boundary with external code using a different convention (e.g. a wrapper around a library that raises) may use the secondary convention, but must be individually marked with a # CONVENTION: secondary — [reason] comment, and their callers must handle both conventions explicitly. The two conventions must never be mixed within a single function. (a) Return-sentinel convention. The function returns None to signal failure. For functions whose normal return value may itself be None, a named module-level sentinel constant must be defined and returned instead (e.g., INVALID = object()). Callers must check for the sentinel per Rule 7. (b) Propagating-exception convention. The function raises a module-specific exception type. This exception must not be caught within the same function — it must propagate to the caller. Callers must handle or explicitly re-raise it per Rule 7.
Data objects must be declared at the smallest possible level of scope.
The return value of non-None functions must be checked by each calling function, and the validity of parameters must be checked inside each function.
The use of metaprogramming must be limited to standard imports and to decorators from the Python standard library only, such as @staticmethod, @classmethod, @property, @functools.wraps, and @dataclasses.dataclass. Dynamic code execution (eval, exec) and recursive decorator patterns are not allowed. All metaprogramming constructs must expand into complete syntactic units. Variadic arguments (*args, **kwargs) may only be used to forward calls to a known, fixed interface and must not be used to define a function's own parameter contract. The use of conditional imports is often also dubious, but cannot always be avoided. This means that there should rarely be justification for more than one or two conditional imports even in large software development efforts, beyond the standard boilerplate that avoids circular imports. Each such use should be flagged by a tool-based checker and justified in the code.
All code must be developed, from the first day of development, with all linter and type-checker warnings enabled at the most pedantic setting. All code must pass these checks without any warnings. All code must be checked daily with at least one, but preferably more than one, state-of-the-art static source code analyzer and should pass the analyses with zero warnings.
Additionally, make sure that the code is modularized and grouped in shared files based on same functionalities. Also, leave comments. Just enough so that the reader can understand. Keep them short and concise.

subagent Selection
Select Deepseek V4 Flash for simple tasks and Deepseek V4 Pro for complex tasks.
